source_paper_title: Linear Program-Based Policies for Restless Bandits: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for (Exponentially Fast) Asymptotic Optimality
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Gast_2024, title={Linear Program-Based Policies for Restless Bandits: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for (Exponentially Fast) Asymptotic Optimality}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0101}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0101}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Gast, Nicolas and Gaujal, Bruno and Yan, Chen}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2468–2491} }
context:
Section 4.3.2 (page 16–18, around the end of the section containing 'We actually conjecture that ...')

We actually conjecture that for “essentially all” degenerate RBs, if \(\pi\) is a LP-compatible policy, then there exists constants \(C, C>0\) independent of \(N\) such that
\[\frac{C}{\sqrt{N}} \le \bigl|V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0), T) - V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0), T)\bigr| \le \frac{C}{\sqrt{N}}.\]
This implies that as long as the RB is degenerate, we can not expect a faster than square root convergence to the LP relaxation bound, for any admissible policy.
title: Prove square-root lower bound for LP-compatible policies in degenerate restless bandits
background_latex:
Consider a discrete-time finite-horizon restless bandit with a population of \(N\) statistically identical arms. Each arm has a finite state space \(S=\{1,\dots,d\}\) and two actions \(a\in\{0,1\}\) (passive/active). If an arm is in state \(s\) and action \(a\) is chosen, an immediate reward \(R_s^a\in\mathbb{R}\) is earned and the arm transitions to state \(s'\) with probability \(P^{a}_{s,s'}\). Arms evolve independently conditional on actions.

At each time \(t=0,1,\dots,T-1\), exactly a fraction \(\alpha\in(0,1)\) of arms is activated (with the convention that if \(\alpha N\) is not an integer, the policy randomizes between \(\lfloor \alpha N\rfloor\) and \(\lfloor \alpha N\rfloor+1\) active arms so that the expected number of active arms equals \(\alpha N\)). Let \(M^{(N)}(t)\in\Delta_d\) be the empirical distribution of arm states at time \(t\), where \(M^{(N)}_s(t)\) is the fraction of arms in state \(s\). Let \(Y^{(N)}(t)\in\Delta_{2d}\) be the empirical state-action fractions, where \(Y^{(N)}_{s,a}(t)\) is the fraction of arms in state \(s\) assigned action \(a\) at time \(t\). The per-arm reward of a policy \(\pi\) is
\[V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0),T):=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Big[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\sum_{s\in S}\sum_{a\in\{0,1\}} R_s^a\,Y^{(N)}_{s,a}(t)\Big].\]

The standard LP relaxation replaces the hard per-time activation constraint by an equality constraint on expected activation. Writing \(y_{s,a}(t):=\mathbb{E}[Y^{(N)}_{s,a}(t)]\), the relaxation value \(V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0),T)\) is the optimum of
\[\max_{y\ge 0}\ \sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\sum_{s,a} R_s^a\,y_{s,a}(t)\]
subject to flow conservation
\[y_{s,0}(t+1)+y_{s,1}(t+1)=\sum_{s'\in S}\sum_{a\in\{0,1\}} y_{s',a}(t)P^{a}_{s',s}\quad \forall s,\ t,\]
activation budget
\[\sum_{s\in S} y_{s,1}(t)=\alpha\quad\forall t,\]
and initial condition \(y_{s,0}(0)+y_{s,1}(0)=m_s(0)\).

Call a Markovian admissible policy \(\pi=\{\pi_t\}_{t=0}^{T-1}\) a map \(\pi_t:\Delta_d\to\Delta_{2d}\) that, given state distribution \(m\), prescribes fractions \(y=\pi_t(m)\) satisfying \(y_{s,a}\ge 0\), \(\sum_a y_{s,a}=m_s\) for all \(s\), and \(\sum_s y_{s,1}=\alpha\). A policy is LP-compatible if there exists an optimal LP solution \(\{y^*(t)\}_{t=0}^{T-1}\) such that \(\pi_t(m^*(t))=y^*(t)\) for all \(t\), where \(m^*_s(t)=y^*_{s,0}(t)+y^*_{s,1}(t)\).

Let \(S_0(t):=\{s\in S: y^*_{s,0}(t)>0\text{ and } y^*_{s,1}(t)>0\}\). The model is called degenerate if for every optimal LP solution there exists at least one time \(t\) such that \(|S_0(t)|=0\), i.e., at that time the LP solution activates some states fully and leaves the rest fully passive with no state partially split between actions.

The paper provides an example showing that for some degenerate models, the LP-relaxation gap under any LP-compatible policy cannot decay faster than order \(1/\sqrt{N}\), and formulates a general conjecture that this phenomenon is typical for degenerate models.
problem_definition_latex:
For a fixed finite-horizon restless bandit instance \((S,\{P^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\{R^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\alpha,T,m(0))\) that is degenerate in the sense that for some time \(t\) every optimal solution \(\{y^*(\tau)\}_{\tau=0}^{T-1}\) of the LP relaxation satisfies \(|S_0(t)|=0\), determine whether the following statement holds (as conjectured in the paper):

There exist constants \(\underline C,\overline C>0\) (depending on the instance but not on \(N\)) such that for every LP-compatible admissible policy \(\pi\) and all sufficiently large \(N\),
\[ \frac{\underline C}{\sqrt{N}}\ \le\ \bigl|V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0),T)-V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0),T)\bigr|\ \le\ \frac{\overline C}{\sqrt{N}}. \]
In particular, the task is to prove or disprove the \(\Omega(1/\sqrt{N})\) lower bound on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for LP-compatible policies on (essentially all) degenerate finite-horizon restless bandits.

For a fixed finite-horizon restless bandit instance \((S,\{P^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\{R^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\alpha,T,m(0))\) that is degenerate in the sense that for some time \(t\) every optimal solution \(\{y^*(\tau)\}_{\tau=0}^{T-1}\) of the LP relaxation satisfies \(|S_0(t)|=0\), determine whether the following statement holds (as conjectured in the paper):

location_in_paper: Section 4.3.2 (page 16–18, around the end of the section containing 'We actually conjecture that ...')
open_problem_quote:
We actually conjecture that for “essentially all” degenerate RBs, if \(\pi\) is a LP-compatible policy, then there exists constants \(C, C>0\) independent of \(N\) such that
\[\frac{C}{\sqrt{N}} \le \bigl|V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0), T) - V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0), T)\bigr| \le \frac{C}{\sqrt{N}}.\]
This implies that as long as the RB is degenerate, we can not expect a faster than square root convergence to the LP relaxation bound, for any admissible policy.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The conjecture concerns scaling of an optimality gap with N; for any fixed small-state model one can simulate or compute the induced finite-N performance of specific LP-compatible policies and estimate the gap numerically. Automated search over small d, T and rational transition probabilities could plausibly uncover degenerate instances that violate the claimed \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound (or suggest refinements).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (small restless-bandit instances and LP-compatible policies/tie-breaking rules) can be represented programmatically, and a fitness signal can be based on computed/simulated gaps \(|V^{(N)}_{\pi}-V_{\mathrm{rel}}|\) over a range of N. However, verifying the universal quantifiers ('for every LP-compatible policy' and asymptotic bounds) is difficult computationally; the loop is more likely to find informative examples or sharpened conjectures than to resolve the statement.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Gast--Gaujal--Yan (2023), finite-horizon homogeneous restless bandits are analyzed via the relaxed LP (flow constraints plus per-time expected activation). A key dichotomy is between non-degenerate instances (every optimal LP solution has at least one split state at every time) where locally linear LP-compatible policies exist and yield exponentially small gaps to \(V_{\mathrm{rel}}\), and degenerate instances (some time \(t\) has \(|S_0(t)|=0\)) where the paper gives a concrete example with an unavoidable \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) gap for LP-compatible policies and conjectures this is typical. The open problem asks to prove a universal \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound (and matching \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) upper bound) for the LP-relaxation gap of any LP-compatible policy on essentially all degenerate finite-horizon instances.

Forward citations provide partial evidence but no general resolution. The strongest supporting result is the Gaussian-approximation line of work ("Achieving O (1/N) optimality gap in restless bandits through Gaussian approximation" / "Achieving Optimality Gap in Restless Bandits through Gaussian Approximation"), which proves that for a specific degenerate example, every policy in a broad fluid/LP-tracking class has \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) optimality gap and that the LP upper bound itself can be \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) above the true \(N\)-arm optimum. This bolsters the view that when the optimal LP trajectory lies on a boundary (as in \(|S_0(t)|=0\)), CLT-scale fluctuations in the empirical state can force a square-root loss for policies that track the LP prescription. At the same time, that work also shows that second-order (Gaussian/stochastic-programming) policies can achieve \(\tilde O(1/N)\) gaps to the true optimum under a uniqueness condition, indicating that the \(1/\sqrt N\) obstruction is tied to LP/fluid compatibility rather than to the RMAB itself.

Other forward citations mainly develop complementary upper-bound theories in infinite-horizon average-reward settings (e.g., MPC/LP-update policies with \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) gaps under mixing and exponential gaps under nondegeneracy/stability; focus-set/ID policies with \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) gaps under unichain/aperiodicity), and general convex-optimization sensitivity tools that explain why degeneracy blocks second-order improvements. A few works on weakly coupled multi-action systems claim exponential convergence to relaxed LP values without nondegeneracy under Lipschitz-type conditions, but these conditions plausibly exclude the discontinuities inherent in the degenerate \(|S_0(t)|=0\) regime. Overall, the conjectured universal \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound for all LP-compatible policies across essentially all degenerate finite-horizon instances remains open; existing results establish \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bounds for substantial LP/fluid-tracking policy classes on explicit degenerate examples and suggest that proving universality will require a general argument linking boundary optimal LP solutions to unavoidable CLT-scale rounding/mismatch losses.

sources.title: Achieving O (1/N) optimality gap in restless bandits through Gaussian approximation
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves two main asymptotic statements for finite-horizon homogeneous RMABs. (i) Under Assumption 4.1 (the fluid LP has a unique optimal solution), the proposed Gaussian-approximation stochastic-programming policy \(\tilde\pi_{N,*}\) achieves \(\tilde O(1/N)\) optimality gap to the true \(N\)-system optimum: \(V^{N}_{\mathrm{opt}}(x_{\mathrm{ini}},1)-V^{N}_{\tilde\pi_{N,*}}(x_{\mathrm{ini}},1)=\tilde O(1/N)\) (Theorem 4.1). (ii) There exist degenerate instances for which every ‘fluid/LP-based’ policy \(\pi\) in the class \(\Pi_{\mathrm{fluid}}(y^*)\) has \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) optimality gap, and moreover the fluid LP upper bound itself is loose by \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\): \(V^{LP}(x_{\mathrm{ini}},1)-V^{N}_{\mathrm{opt}}(x_{\mathrm{ini}},1)=\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) (Theorem 4.2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound (and matching \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) upper bound) on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for every LP-compatible policy in degenerate finite-horizon RMABs. This paper does not prove such a universal lower bound. Instead it provides: - Evidence supporting the conjectured phenomenon by proving a \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound for a broad class of LP-based policies \(\Pi_{\mathrm{fluid}}(y^*)\) on a specific degenerate example (Theorem 4.2), and showing the LP bound itself can be \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) away from the true optimum on that example. - A contrasting positive result: for degenerate problems satisfying a uniqueness condition on the fluid LP solution, there exists a non-LP-compatible (SP-based) policy with much smaller gap \(\tilde O(1/N)\) to the true optimum (Theorem 4.1). This does not refute the conjecture as stated (which is about LP-compatible policies), but it shows degeneracy does not inherently force \(1/\sqrt N\) gaps for all computationally efficient policies, and it suggests the obstruction is tied to the fluid/LP approximation rather than the RMAB itself. Overall: partial progress—establishes \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bounds for a large LP-based class on an example, but not for all LP-compatible policies nor for all degenerate instances.

sources.title: Multi-Action Restless Bandits with Weakly Coupled Constraints: Simultaneous Learning and Control
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves exponential-in-system-size performance deviation bounds for a class of linear-programming-based (ALP/OALP) policies for finite-horizon weakly coupled restless bandit/MDP systems, without requiring the non-degeneracy assumptions used in prior LP-based asymptotic optimality results. Concretely, for scaling parameter \(h\) (with \(N_i=hN_i^0\)), it establishes concentration of the empirical state–action occupancy process \(Z^{\varphi,h}(t)\) around a deterministic fluid/averaging trajectory \(z^{\varphi}(t)\): for any fixed \(T<\infty\) and \(\epsilon>0\), there exist \(C,H>0\) such that for all \(h>H\), \[\mathbb P\Big(\max_{t=0,\dots,T}\|Z^{\varphi,h}(t)-z^{\varphi}(t)\|>\epsilon\Big)\le e^{-Ch}.\] Using this, it claims that adapted LP policies \(\psi\) satisfying a Lipschitz-type condition (their (67)) achieve \(\|z^{\psi,h}(t)-z^{\bar\varphi^*,h}(t)\|\le C_1 e^{-C_2 h}+\epsilon\) uniformly over \(t\le T\), where \(\bar\varphi^*\) is induced by an optimal solution of the relaxed LP; hence the per-unit reward gap to the relaxed optimum is \(O(e^{-h})\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for LP-compatible policies in *degenerate* finite-horizon restless bandits (where optimal LP solutions have no partially split states at some time). This paper instead develops a general framework (weakly coupled gangs) and proves/claims *exponentially small* \(O(e^{-h})\) performance deviation for a class of LP-based policies (ALP/OALP) relative to the relaxed LP optimum, explicitly stating that their exponential bound does not rely on non-degeneracy of the LP solution. If correct and applicable to the same model class as Gast–Gaujal–Yan (2023), this would contradict the conjectured \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound—at least for the policies they analyze—because exponential decay is asymptotically much smaller than \(1/\sqrt N\). However, their ALP/OALP policies are not shown (in the provided text) to be "LP-compatible" in the specific sense of the open problem (matching an optimal LP solution \(y^*(t)\) on the fluid trajectory), and their key sufficient condition (67) is a Lipschitz closeness of action fractions to the relaxed policy as a function of state-occupancy \(\Upsilon\), which may fail precisely in degenerate cases where the LP optimizer induces discontinuous/tie-breaking action maps. Thus the paper provides partial progress/related evidence: it suggests exponential gaps can occur even without non-degeneracy assumptions, but it does not directly establish or refute the \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound for *all* LP-compatible policies in degenerate models.

sources.title: Model predictive control is almost optimal for restless bandits
sources.theorem_or_result:
For an infinite-horizon average-reward RMAB with per-period hard budget 
\(\sum_i u_i(t)\le \alpha\) and empirical state dynamics \(x(t+1)=x(t)P^0+u(t)(P^1-P^0)\), the paper analyzes the rolling-horizon LP/MPC policy (LP-update) that at each time solves a \(\tau\)-horizon linear program and applies the first control with randomized rounding. Under a mixing/ergodicity condition (Assumption 1: \(\rho_k>0\) for some \(k\)), it proves an \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) optimality gap for the resulting policy relative to the optimal \(N\)-arm average reward:
- Theorem 4.1: for any \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\tau(\varepsilon)\) such that
\[V^{(N)}_{\mathrm{LP},\tau(\varepsilon)}(\infty)\ge V^{N}_{\mathrm{opt}}(\infty)-\varepsilon - O(1/N) - O(1/\sqrt N).\]
Under additional nondegeneracy and local stability assumptions (Assumptions 2–4), it strengthens this to exponential closeness:
- Theorem 4.2: if \(\alpha N\in\mathbb{Z}\), then
\[V^{(N)}_{\mathrm{LP},\tau(\varepsilon)}(\infty)\ge V^{N}_{\mathrm{opt}}(\infty)-\varepsilon - C'e^{-C''N}.\]
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a *lower bound* \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for *LP-compatible* policies in *degenerate finite-horizon* RMABs (where optimal LP solutions have times with no partially split states). This paper does not address that conjecture directly: it studies an *infinite-horizon average-reward* setting and provides *upper bounds* on the suboptimality of a specific rolling-horizon LP/MPC policy relative to the true \(N\)-arm optimum.

Relevance: it supports the broader narrative that \(1/\sqrt N\) is the generic rate without additional stability/nondegeneracy (Theorem 4.1 gives an \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) upper bound “in general”), and it explicitly contrasts this with exponential rates under a *nondegenerate* fixed point (Assumption 2) and stability. However, it neither proves that degeneracy forces a \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) *lower* bound, nor does it analyze the finite-horizon LP-compatibility notion from Gast–Gaujal–Yan (2023b) in the degenerate regime. In fact, its exponential result assumes nondegeneracy, which is essentially the opposite of the degenerate case in the open problem.

sources.title: Achieving exponential asymptotic optimality in average-reward restless bandits without global attractor assumption
sources.theorem_or_result: In the infinite-horizon average-reward restless bandit with homogeneous arms, the paper proves: (i) under Assumptions 1 (aperiodic unichain of the LP-induced single-arm chain), 2 (non-degeneracy: a unique neutral state with both actions used in an optimal LP solution), and 3 (local stability: all eigenvalues of the linearized matrix \(\Phi\) satisfy \(|\lambda(\Phi)|<1\)), the proposed two-set policy \(\pi\) achieves an exponential gap to the LP upper bound \(R_{\mathrm{rel}}\): \(R_{\mathrm{rel}}-R(\pi,S_0)=O(e^{-CN})\) for some \(C>0\) independent of \(N\) (Theorem 1). (ii) For ‘regular unstable’ instances (violating local stability: \(\Phi\) has an eigenvalue with modulus \(>1\), plus mild regularity/noise conditions), every policy \(\pi\) satisfies a CLT-order lower bound \(R_{\mathrm{rel}}-R(\pi,S_0)=\Omega(1/\sqrt{N})\) (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for LP-compatible policies in *degenerate finite-horizon* restless bandits, where at some time \(t\) the optimal finite-horizon LP has no split states (\(|S_0(t)|=0\)). This paper does not address that finite-horizon degeneracy setting. Instead it studies *infinite-horizon average reward* and assumes *non-degeneracy* (existence of a neutral/split state) to obtain exponential gaps, i.e., it explicitly excludes the degenerate case of interest. It does, however, prove an \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound in a different regime: when local stability fails (a linearized dynamics matrix \(\Phi\) is unstable), then no policy can get closer than order \(1/\sqrt N\) to the LP upper bound \(R_{\mathrm{rel}}\). This provides partial progress/related evidence that CLT-order lower bounds arise from structural obstructions, but it neither proves nor disproves the conjectured \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound for LP-compatible policies in *degenerate* models as defined in the original finite-horizon paper.

sources.title: Unichain and aperiodicity are sufficient for asymptotic optimality of average-reward restless bandits
sources.theorem_or_result: Under Assumption 1 (existence of an optimal LP solution whose induced optimal single-armed policy \(\bar\pi^*\) yields an aperiodic unichain Markov chain), the paper constructs “focus-set” policies (including the set-expansion policy and the ID policy) for the discrete-time infinite-horizon average-reward restless bandit with \(N\) homogeneous arms and exact per-time budget \(\alpha N\), and proves these policies are asymptotically optimal with optimality gap \(R^*(N,S_0)-R(\pi,S_0)=O(1/\sqrt N)\).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns *finite-horizon* restless bandits and asks for a universal \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) *lower bound* on the LP-relaxation gap for any LP-compatible policy in *degenerate* instances (where optimal LP solutions have no partially split states at some time). This citing paper instead studies *infinite-horizon average-reward* RBs and provides *upper bounds* \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) for new policies under an aperiodic-unichain assumption, without analyzing degeneracy-induced lower bounds. It does discuss non-degeneracy assumptions in prior work as enabling exponential gaps, but does not prove that degeneracy forces \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) gaps, nor does it address the finite-horizon LP-compatible class in the conjecture.

sources.title: Certainty equivalence control-based heuristics in multi-stage convex stochastic optimization problems
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper develops certainty-equivalence-control (CEC) relaxations for multi-stage convex stochastic optimization with hard constraints and proposes two feasible heuristics (update/re-solving and projection) derived from the relaxed convex program. It proves upper bounds on the suboptimality gap: under local Lipschitz regularity of the relevant solution/projection mappings, the gap is bounded by a constant times \(\sqrt{\mathrm{var}[W]}+\sqrt{\mathrm{var}[E]}\); under additional local \(C^2\) smoothness (nondegeneracy/LICQ-type conditions), the bound improves to order \(\mathrm{var}[W]+\mathrm{var}[E]\) (Theorems 1–4). In the Markovian bandit scaling where \(\mathrm{var}[E]=O(1/N)\), this yields an \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) bound in general and an \(O(1/N)\) bound under nondegeneracy.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a universal \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for LP-compatible policies in *degenerate* finite-horizon restless bandits. This paper does not prove any lower bounds in \(N\) for degenerate bandits, nor does it analyze LP-compatible policies in the sense of matching an optimal LP solution along the fluid trajectory. Instead it provides a general framework and *upper* bounds for CEC-based heuristics, and it explicitly links faster \(O(1/N)\) rates to nondegeneracy/smoothness, while noting that degeneracy can prevent second-order improvements (e.g., projection mapping degenerate in their numerics). Thus it supplies sensitivity-analysis tools and a conceptual explanation consistent with the conjecture (degeneracy \(\Rightarrow\) only first-order \(O(1/\sqrt N)\) behavior), but it neither establishes the conjectured \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound nor the matching \(\Theta(1/\sqrt N)\) characterization for all LP-compatible policies.
